data.title: Hello Group reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Hello Group press release (NASDAQ:MOMO): Q1 Non-GAAP EPADS of $0.32.
  • Revenue of $347.4M (-2.0% Y/Y) beats by $11.93M.
  • Diluted net income per American Depositary Share ("ADS") was RMB2.07 (US$0.29) in the first quarter of 2025, compared to RMB0.03 in the same period of 2024.
  • Monthly Active Users ("MAU") on Tantan app were 10.7 million in March 2025, compared to 13.7 million in March 2024.
  • For the Momo app total paying users was 4.2 million for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 7.1 million for the same period last year. Tantan had 0.8 million paying users for the first quarter of 2025 compared to 1.1 million from the year ago period.
  • For the second quarter of 2025, the Company expects total net revenues to be between RMB2.57 billion to RMB2.67 billion, representing a decrease of 4.5% to 0.8% year over year. This forecast reflects the Company's current and preliminary views on the market and operational conditions, which are subject to change.

data.title: Auto industry braces for supply chain disruptions from China's rare earth curbs
data.description:
The automotive industry is bracing for supply chain disruptions due to China's export restrictions on rare earth elements and magnets, which were imposed in retaliation to President Donald Trump's tariffs.

China dominates the critical mineral supply chain, producing about 90% of the world's rare earths that are widely used in the auto, energy, semiconductor, and defense industries.

"The European automotive supply industry is already experiencing significant disruption," auto supplier association CLEPA has warned, adding that the impacted auto parts are critical to combustion engine and electric vehicles. 

"These restrictions have led to the shutdown of several production lines and plants across Europe, with further impacts expected in the coming weeks as inventories deplete," the group noted.

While the European Union is reportedly in talks with China to address the issue, the bloc is looking to diversify supply chains. The EU on Wednesday identified 13 critical raw material projects overseas to ensure sustainable supply, including in the UK, Ukraine, Greenland, Canada, and South Africa.

Industry impact

Some of BMW's (OTCPK:BAMXF) supplier network has been disrupted by China's export controls, but the automaker's own plants are running as normal, Reuters reported.

Joerg Burzer, production chief at Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF), said the company is not currently impacted, but it is discussing with suppliers possible "physical buffers" for its supply chain.

A Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) spokesperson told CNBC that while the automaker's supply chain is stable, its suppliers have indicated that only a limited number of rare earth export licenses have been granted by Chinese authorities.

In a conference on Wednesday, Ford (NYSE:F) CFO Sherry House said there have been some delays in procuring rare earths, but the company is managing the issue for now. "It just puts stress on a system that's highly organized, with parts being ordered many weeks in advance."

Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) CEO Ivan Espinosa told CNBC that the automaker is looking at ways to minimize the impact of the export controls. Suzuki (OTCPK:SZKMY) has reportedly halted production of its Swift car temporarily.

data.title: European indexes flat as investors await ECB interest rate decision
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.01% to 8,802, staying flat for the fourth straight day as mixed corporate news weigh investor sentiment.

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.12% to 24,315, surpassing 24,300 to hit a new milestone and extend its winning streak to three consecutive days. The HCOB Germany Construction PMI slipped to 44.4 in May. Factory orders in Germany rose 0.6% M/M in April.

France (CAC:IND) +0.15% to 7,816, The HCOB Construction PMI in France fell to 43.1 in May.

The HCOB Eurozone Construction PMI fell to 45.6 in May.

The HCOB Italy Construction PMI edged up to 50.5 in May.

Finland’s trade deficit narrowed to €0.10B in April. Slovenia posted a trade deficit of €446.5M in April.

Sweden’s current account surplus widened sharply to SEK 119.3B in Q1.

The Swiss unemployment rate remained stable at a non-seasonally adjusted 2.8% in May.

Wholesale prices in Austria fell by 0.5% Y/Y in May.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) gained 0.21% to 552.2, as investors refrained from making big bets ahead of the European Central Bank’s monetary policy decision later in the day. European natural gas futures climbed above €36 per megawatt-hour as supply from Norway, the region’s top gas exporter, dropped due to planned maintenance at the Kollsnes processing plant.

Coming up in the session: ECB interest rate decision is lined up for the day, where it expected to cut interest rates by another 25 basis points at its June meeting as it seeks to support growth amid slowing inflation and ongoing global trade uncertainties.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 1 basis point to 4.38%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 1 basis point to 2.53%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 2 basis points to 4.62%. 

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Suzuki Motor halted Swift production due to China's rare earth curbs - report
data.description:
Suzuki Motor (OTCPK:SZKMY) had suspended production of its Swift car due to China's rare earth restrictions, becoming the first Japanese automaker to be affected by the export curbs, Reuters reported on Thursday.

It had announced the planned production stoppage of the Swift subcompact, excluding the Swift Sport model, from May 26 through June 6, citing a shortage of components.

Suzuki Motor (OTCPK:SZKMY) did not elaborate beyond that, and a spokesperson said the company had no comment on the reason for the suspension, which was first reported by the Nikkei business daily.

Suzuki would partially restart production of the Swift cars at its Sagara plant on June 13, resuming fully after June 16, as the "prospect of parts supply is clearer" now, it said in a later statement on Thursday.

Several European auto parts factories have also halted production, while Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF) is exploring strategies to safeguard against potential rare earth shortages.

data.title: Canada to hold off response on higher U.S. metals tariffs
data.description:
As U.S. President Donald Trump formally signed an order doubling tariffs on imported steel and aluminum to 50%, Canada—the largest supplier of these metals to the U.S.—remains significantly impacted. Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada will hold off on immediate retaliation against the higher tariffs.

Carney described these U.S. hikes in levies as "illegal" and "unjustified," emphasizing the harm to both American and Canadian industries.

The decision to pause further retaliatory action is due to ongoing negotiations between Ottawa and Washington on a new bilateral economic and security agreement.

“We will take some time—not much, some time—because we are in intensive discussions right now with the Americans on our trading relationship,” Carney said when asked about his government’s response. “Those discussions are progressing. I would note that the American action is global action. It’s not one targeted at Canada. So we’ll take some time, but not more.”

However, if the negotiations with the U.S. fail, the government plans to retaliate, Carney said in the House of Commons.

Carney's cabinet point person for U.S. trade negotiations, Dominic LeBlanc, met with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick in Washington on June 3, marking his second visit to the U.S. capital in two weeks.

Major Canadian labor unions and industry groups, like the Canadian Steel Producers Association, are urging the government to impose matching or higher tariffs on U.S. steel and aluminum imports.

“At a 25 per cent tariff rate, we saw significant layoffs, curtailed investments and a significant drop of shipments to the United States,” said Catherine Cobden, CEO of the Canadian Steel Producers Association. “At a 50 per cent tariff rate, the U.S. market is effectively closed to Canadian steel, leaving billions of dollars of Canadian steel without a market." 

"We can't ship at 50 per cent. Perhaps we can stockpile for a few days, but obviously we can't keep producing if one of our major markets is shuttered." she added.

Canadian Labour Congress president Bea Bruske said the government should meet “force with force” by imposing surtaxes and blocking U.S. steel and aluminum.

List of ETFs for Canadian markets: (BATS:BBCA), (NYSEARCA:EWC), (NYSEARCA:FLCA), (NYSEARCA:FXC).

data.title: Honeywell, Johnson Matthey, Gidara Energy and Samsung E&A form saf technology alliance
data.description:
Honeywell (NASDAQ:HON), Johnson Matthey (OTCPK:JMPLF), Gidara Energy, and Samsung E&A announced on Thursday the formation of a strategic technology alliance aimed at delivering a comprehensive, end-to-end global solution for producing sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) from biomass and municipal solid waste.

This collaboration combines the partners’ diverse expertise to help customers optimize every stage of the SAF production process—from feedstock sourcing to final fuel output.

The joint approach aims to expedite project timelines and provide customers with a single point of accountability for project execution and product guarantees, ensuring communication, coordination and reliability throughout the SAF production process.

With the resources of all four companies, the integrated and modular solution is expected to reduce the time between feasibility study and facility startup by more than 15% and can reduce capital expenditures by up to 10%, the companies said.

data.title: Chile and Google sign agreement to deploy a trans-Pacific submarine cable
data.description:
Chile and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) signed an agreement on Wednesday to install the first submarine fiber optic cable connecting South America with Asia and Oceania, with completion expected by 2027.

The initiative is being launched almost a decade after it was first proposed in 2016, and six years after the initial studies to determine its feasibility.

“The idea of ​​building this cable is that it can also be used not only by Google but also by other users, such as technology companies operating in Chile,” said Cristian Ramos, director of telecommunications infrastructure for Latin America at Alphabet, Google’s parent company.

While Google did not reveal the total amount of its investment, Patricio Rey, general manager of Desarrollo País—a state-owned infrastructure company partnering on the project—estimated the cable's value at between $300 million and $550 million, with Chile contributing $25 million.

data.title: Citigroup to cut 3,500 jobs at China tech centers
Global energy investment to touch record $3.3 trillion in 2025, IEA says
data.description:
Global energy investment is projected to reach a record $3.3 trillion in 2025, driven primarily by a surge in clean energy spending, according to a new International Energy Agency (IEA) report. The figure is a 2% rise in real terms compared to 2024, despite ongoing economic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions worldwide.

About $2.2 trillion will be invested in clean energy technologies, which include renewables, nuclear, energy storage, which is twice the amount for fossil fuels.

Solar power is the largest beneficiary, with investments forecasted to hit $450 billion in 2025, making it the top energy technology globally in terms of capital allocation. Investment in battery storage is expected to reach around $65-66 billion. Capital flows to nuclear power have grown by 50% over the past five years and are on course to reach around $75 billion in 2025.

In contrast, investment in oil and gas is expected to decline, with upstream oil investment set to fall by 6% in 2025.

Spending on electricity grids is projected to surpass $400 billion in 2025 but is still lagging the pace of growth in generation and electrification. Maintaining electricity security would require investment in grids to rise towards parity with generation spending by the early 2030s.

“Amid the geopolitical and economic uncertainties that are clouding the outlook for the energy world, we see energy security coming through as a key driver of the growth in global investment this year to a record $3.3 trillion as countries and companies seek to insulate themselves from a wide range of risks,” said IEA Executive Director Fatih Birol. 

“The fast-evolving economic and trade picture means that some investors are adopting a wait-and-see approach to new energy project approvals, but in most areas we have yet to see significant implications for existing projects.”

Despite the positive trends, there are challenges; spending patterns vary globally, with many developing economies struggling to mobilize capital for energy infrastructure.

Here is a list of ETFs tracking the energy sector: (NYSEARCA:USO), (NYSEARCA:BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (NYSEARCA:SCO), (NYSEARCA:USL), (NYSEARCA:DBO), (NYSEARCA:DRIP), (NYSEARCA:GUSH), (NASDAQ:USOI), (NYSEARCA:UNG), (NYSEARCA:BOIL), (NYSEARCA:KOLD), (NYSEARCA:UNL), (NYSEARCA:FCG).

And here is a list tracking clean energy: (NASDAQ:ICLN), (NASDAQ:QCLN), (NYSEARCA:PBW), (NYSEARCA:LCTD), (NASDAQ:NZAC), (NYSEARCA:SMOG), (NYSEARCA:CNRG), (NYSEARCA:SOLR).

data.title: Five Below outlines $4.33B–$4.42B sales target amid tariff mitigation and strategic sourcing shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five Below (FIVE) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Winifred Y. Park opened the call highlighting that "we had a strong first quarter with financial results that exceeded our expectations," attributing outperformance to a "heightened focus on the customer and working as a tight knit multidisciplinary team from merchandising, planning and allocations to marketing, store operations and supply chain." Park emphasized broad-based sales outperformance across product categories, citing product innovation in areas such as trend-right beauty, novelty, food and candy, and tech accessories.

  • Park stated, "Sales and comps exceeded our updated guidance with sales of $971 million and comparable sales increase of 7.1%." She noted a 6.2% rise in transactions, supported by 55 new store openings in 20 states. Two openings, in Victorville, California and Joplin, Missouri, ranked among the top 25 all-time grand openings for the company.

  • The company continued investing in store experience, adding labor and improving processes, which Park said were "paying off" and positioning stores to "assist customers while also ensuring our shelves are stocked with trend-right products."

  • Park addressed the tariff environment, stating, "Our plans include vendor negotiations, diversification of sourcing, continued investment in new value pack product as well as assortment and pricing adjustments with a focus on reducing the number of price points." She highlighted a "reduction in goods sourced from China by about 10 percentage points for the back half of the year."

  • CFO Kristy Chipman remarked, "Total sales in the first quarter of 2025 increased 19.5% to $970.5 million from $811.9 million in the first quarter last year. Comparable sales increased 7.1%, driven by an increase in comp transactions of 6.2% and comp ticket of 0.9%." Chipman announced her departure for personal reasons, with an interim CFO appointed pending a national search for her replacement.

Outlook

  • COO Kenneth R. Bull provided guidance reflecting tariff impacts and current performance. "For the second quarter of 2025, we expect total sales in the range of $975 million to $995 million or growth of 18.7% at the midpoint versus last year's second quarter. Comparable sales are expected to increase between 7% and 9%."

  • Adjusted diluted earnings per share for Q2 is expected between $0.50 and $0.62. For the full year, "sales are expected to be in the range of $4.33 billion to $4.42 billion, with a comparable sales increase of 3% to 5%." Bull noted, "The midpoint of our full year operating margin guidance is unchanged from our prior outlook at approximately 7.3%."

  • The impact of tariffs is expected to largely offset improved sales performance in operating profit, with mitigation efforts ongoing.

Financial Results

  • Sales for Q1 2025 were $970.5 million, with comparable sales up 7.1%. Adjusted gross profit was $328.4 million, up 24.6%. Adjusted gross margin rose to 33.8%, an increase of approximately 140 basis points. Adjusted SG&A for the quarter was 27.7% of sales.

  • Adjusted operating income reached $59.6 million and adjusted net income was $47.5 million, resulting in adjusted diluted earnings per share of $0.86. The company ended the quarter with approximately $624 million in cash and no debt.

  • Inventory was $702 million, with per-store inventory down about 2% year-over-year due to prior write-downs. Inventory levels are expected to be "significantly higher" at the end of Q2 as the company accelerates receipts in response to global trade shifts.

Q&A

  • Michael Lasser, UBS, asked about the drivers behind the "remarkable pivot in the comp cadence." Park credited "the actions that we've taken are definitively paying off," citing product selection, value, inventory flow, and improved store labor.

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan, queried the source of comp strength. Park pointed to "nice growth, both in terms of new customers and comp stores as well as existing customers returning to us." She highlighted gains in beauty, food, novelty candy, style, and collectibles. Bull added, "We're going to continue to invest in the stores...hours for the stores or the simplification and reduction of workloads."

  • Charles Grom, Gordon Haskett, asked about operating margin compression and tariffs. Bull clarified, "on a full year basis in terms of the impact of tariffs, net of any mitigation activities. We see it as about 150 basis points for the full year."

  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley, asked about pricing and mitigation. Park stated, "We are looking at touching about 15% in terms of price, both up and down. And it's not just for tariffs. It's really looking at relative value in the marketplace." Bull explained, "the assumption there is that any benefit that we would get from pricing adjustments would be offset by unit degradation."

  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo, inquired about margin recapture. Park emphasized, "sales solve problems," adding that tariff challenges have "accelerated work that we're doing on the merchandising front."

  • Multiple questions focused on sourcing diversification and the impact of tariffs, with Park noting a 10 percentage point reduction in China sourcing for the back half and stating, "mitigation efforts and initiatives are embedded in the guidance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin pressure, tariff mitigation, and sustainability of sales momentum, with a tone that is cautiously optimistic but probing, especially on the ability to sustain the comp gains and manage cost headwinds.

  • Management maintained a confident, disciplined tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently citing "strategy," "mitigation," and "execution," but also displayed prudence, particularly in guiding for the back half of the year. Park stated, "We are looking at 2-year stack comps across the next upcoming quarters of plus 2%." Slight caution was evident regarding macroeconomic uncertainty.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed greater confidence regarding sales momentum, though both sides remain wary of margin risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year sales was raised from a previous range of $4.21 billion–$4.33 billion to $4.33 billion–$4.42 billion, with comp sales expectations now 3%–5%, up from flat to 3% previously. Operating margin guidance remains steady at approximately 7.3%.

  • The company reported a sharp improvement in Q1 comparable sales (7.1% growth compared to the prior quarter’s negative comp), with strong transaction growth.

  • Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q4 to increased confidence in Q1, especially around customer engagement and product performance. Analysts continued to focus on margin headwinds, tariffs, and the sustainability of gains, but acknowledged improved execution.

  • The CFO transition was announced this quarter, with Kristy Chipman departing for personal reasons and an interim CFO stepping in while a search is underway.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs remain a major challenge, with management outlining mitigation strategies such as vendor negotiations, sourcing diversification, and pricing adjustments.

  • Operating margin faces pressure from tariffs (estimated at 150 basis points for the year), higher incentive compensation, and continued investments in store labor.

  • Inventory management and the ability to maintain in-stock positions amid supply chain shifts are ongoing concerns.

  • Macro uncertainty and potential consumer demand shifts were cited as reasons for prudence in guidance for the back half of the year.

Final Takeaway

Five Below’s Q1 2025 results highlight strong sales and comp performance driven by customer-focused execution, product innovation, and improved store experience. Management raised full-year sales guidance to $4.33 billion–$4.42 billion and expects comparable sales growth of 3%–5%, while maintaining operating margin targets despite significant tariff headwinds. Mitigation strategies are in place to address sourcing and cost pressures, and the company is navigating leadership changes with a new CFO search underway. The focus remains on sustaining transaction growth, expanding market presence, and delivering value amid a dynamic macro and trade environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
